findings.title: Admin withdraw lacks signer verification
findings.description: The admin_withdraw instruction accepts `admin: AccountInfo<'info>` instead of `Signer<'info>` and performs no `is_signer` check. Any account can be passed as `admin`, allowing an attacker to drain the vault by calling admin_withdraw with their own pubkey as the admin field.
findings.impact: Complete drain of the program vault. No authentication required — any transaction that supplies a destination account can withdraw arbitrary lamports up to the vault's balance.
findings.recommendation: Change `admin: AccountInfo<'info>` to `admin: Signer<'info>` in the AdminWithdraw struct. Anchor will enforce signer verification at deserialization. Add a `has_one = admin` constraint on the vault account to bind the admin to the vault's stored authority.
findings.root_cause: The admin field was declared as AccountInfo instead of Signer. Anchor's deserializer accepts any AccountInfo regardless of whether it signed the transaction. No is_signer gate exists.

findings.title: Hardcoded bump literal in initialize
findings.description: The initialize instruction uses a hardcoded bump literal (254) rather than the canonical bump returned by `find_program_address` or `ctx.bumps.vault`. Storing a non-canonical bump allows an attacker who finds a different valid bump to derive a colliding PDA.
findings.impact: PDA collision. If the stored bump is not the canonical (highest valid) bump, an alternative seed/bump combination may produce a valid PDA at the same address, undermining the program's PDA-based access control.
findings.recommendation: Replace `let _hardcoded_bump: u8 = 254;` with `let bump = ctx.bumps.vault;` and store `ctx.bumps.vault` directly. Anchor 0.30+ canonicalizes bumps automatically. If deriving manually, use `Pubkey::find_program_address(seeds, program_id)` which returns the canonical bump.
findings.root_cause: A non-canonical bump value was hardcoded instead of using the canonical bump from ctx.bumps or find_program_address. Any valid bump produces a PDA, so a non-canonical bump creates collision risk.

findings.title: Arbitrary CPI to user-supplied program
findings.description: The exec_callback instruction accepts a `target_program: AccountInfo` with no allowlist validation and invokes it via `invoke(&ix, ...)`. An attacker can pass any program ID, including the System Program, Token Program, or a malicious program, and craft instruction data and account metas to perform unauthorized operations (e.g., draining token accounts the user has authority over).
findings.impact: Privilege escalation via arbitrary CPI. Attackers can exploit the program's instruction context to perform any signed operation the caller has authority for, including transfers, account closures, and authority changes.
findings.recommendation: Replace `target_program: AccountInfo<'info>` with `target_program: Program<'info, SomeKnownProgram>`, where SomeKnownProgram is an Anchor wrapper around the expected program. If a dynamic program is genuinely required (e.g., plug-in architecture), maintain an on-chain allowlist PDA and verify the target_program key matches a registered entry.
findings.root_cause: The target program ID was accepted as a user-supplied AccountInfo without validation against an allowlist. Any program ID can be invoked, enabling arbitrary CPI to attacker-chosen programs.

findings.title: Lamport drain via unchecked transfer
findings.description: The DrainVault struct uses plain AccountInfo for both vault and destination with no Signer constraint on authority and no has_one constraint binding the destination to a known recipient. The drain_vault function debits vault and credits destination via raw try_borrow_mut_lamports with no validation. An attacker can pass their own address as the destination and drain all vault lamports up to the rent-exempt balance.
findings.impact: Lamport drain. Each call to drain_vault transfers up to the vault's full balance to an attacker-controlled destination. With no rate limit and no authority check, a single transaction empties the vault.
findings.recommendation: Add authority: Signer<'info> to the DrainVault struct, and #[account(has_one = authority)] on the vault field. Verify the destination is derived from authority (e.g., #[account(address = vault.authority)]) rather than accepting user-supplied input. Never perform raw lamport transfers without authority verification.
findings.root_cause: The close target was user-supplied via AccountInfo without authority binding. An attacker can pass their own address as the close destination, receiving the account's lamports.

findings.title: Unchecked arithmetic on user-supplied deposit amount
findings.description: The user_deposit instruction computes `vault.total_deposits = vault.total_deposits + amount` using Rust's default `+` operator on `u64`. In release mode, this wraps silently on overflow. An attacker can deposit an amount that wraps total_deposits back to 0 or to a small number, then withdraw against an inflated balance.
findings.impact: Integer overflow on deposit accounting. The wrapped total_deposits no longer reflects actual deposits, enabling withdrawals up to the (wrapped) recorded balance. Repeated wraps can drain the vault.
findings.recommendation: Use `checked_add`: `vault.total_deposits = vault.total_deposits.checked_add(amount).ok_or(VaultError::Overflow)?;`. Add an `Overflow` variant to the VaultError enum. Apply the same pattern to any other `+`/`-`/`*` on user-controlled amounts.
findings.root_cause: Arithmetic operations used default wrapping semantics (+, -, *, /) on u64 amounts. In Solana release mode, wrapping silently wraps on overflow, corrupting accounting state.

findings.title: Manual init lacks 8-byte discriminator — reinit attack possible
findings.description: The VaultState struct is declared with `#[derive(Clone)]` only, missing `#[account]`. Without the `#[account]` attribute, Anchor does not write the 8-byte discriminator on initialization and does not verify it on deserialization. The Initialize struct uses `AccountInfo` directly, not `Account<'info, VaultState>`, so there is no implicit discriminator check.
findings.impact: Reinitialization attack. An attacker who controls a vault account's key (e.g., previously held lamports that were refunded) can call initialize again and reset the vault's authority to a key they control. This effectively hijacks the vault.
findings.recommendation: Add `#[account]` to the VaultState struct. Change `vault: AccountInfo<'info>` to `vault: Account<'info, VaultState>` (or use `#[account(init, ...)]` with proper constraints). Anchor's Account<'info, T> wrapper verifies the 8-byte discriminator on load, preventing reinit.
findings.root_cause: Initialize wrote account fields without checking the 8-byte Anchor discriminator. A closed (zeroed) account re-funded to rent-exemption is indistinguishable from a freshly-initialized account.

findings.title: Integer division truncation in share calculation
findings.description: The calc_shares function computes `shares = deposit / divisor` using Rust's `/` operator, which truncates toward zero. If divisor exceeds deposit, the result is 0 and the user receives 0 shares for a non-zero deposit. The deposit is not refunded, so the user loses funds silently.
findings.impact: Indirect fund loss via share miscalculation. Users who pass small deposits get 0 shares and lose their deposit. Repeated attacks can drain a pool of misallocated shares.
findings.recommendation: Use checked_div and verify the result against a minimum threshold: `let shares = deposit.checked_div(divisor).ok_or(VaultError::DivisionByZero)?; require!(shares >= MIN_SHARES, VaultError::BelowMinimum);`. For tokens that support fractional units, ensure the divisor is always <= deposit or apply a refund for the remainder.
findings.root_cause: The calc_shares function used Rust's / operator on integer amounts without checked_div. Integer division in Rust truncates toward zero; when divisor exceeds deposit, the result rounds to 0 and the user receives zero shares while the protocol credits the full deposit amount.

findings.title: Off-by-one in threshold check (>= vs >)
findings.description: The check_threshold function uses `>` instead of `>=`, allowing an attacker to bypass the threshold check by exactly hitting the threshold value. If the threshold is 1_000_000, an attacker passing value=1_000_000 passes the gate when the intent was for that to be rejected.
findings.impact: Threshold bypass. Logic that depends on the threshold (e.g., access control, fee tier, rate limit) can be evaded by choosing boundary values.
findings.recommendation: Use `>=` for inclusive thresholds or `>` for exclusive thresholds consistently. Add boundary tests that assert both the threshold value and threshold-1 are handled correctly. Document the intent of each comparison.
findings.root_cause: The check_threshold function used > instead of >=, allowing an attacker to bypass the threshold exactly at the boundary value. This is an off-by-one logic error: the boundary condition is included in the attack surface when it should be excluded.

findings.title: CPI return value discarded (no ? propagation)
findings.description: The unchecked_cpi function discards the result of `invoke()` via `let _ = ...`. If the CPI fails (program error, account not found, etc.), the outer transaction continues and returns Ok(()). State is left inconsistent: the caller thinks the CPI succeeded, but it didn't.
findings.impact: Silent failure of cross-program invocation. Off-chain monitoring cannot detect the failure because the transaction succeeded. State inconsistency between caller and callee can lead to loss of funds or stuck accounts.
findings.recommendation: Propagate CPI errors with `?`: `invoke(&ix, &[])?;`. Add error handling specific to the called program if needed. If silent failure is genuinely intended (rare), document why and emit an event for monitoring.
findings.rule_caught: (non-rule-aligned — Rule 4 covers CPI safety but not result handling)
findings.root_cause: The unchecked_cpi function discarded the Result of invoke() via let _ = ... instead of propagating with ?. When the inner CPI fails (wrong program, bad accounts, insufficient funds), the outer transaction returns Ok(()) and state changes persist without the CPI's intended effect.

findings.title: Missing event emission for withdrawals
findings.description: The silent_withdraw function performs a lamport transfer but emits no structured event. Off-chain indexers (security monitoring, accounting, front-end balance displays) cannot detect or react to withdrawals in real-time.
findings.impact: Audit trail gap. Security incidents cannot be detected by off-chain monitors; users see stale balances; accounting systems miss transactions. In a hack scenario, post-mortem analysis is impossible.
findings.recommendation: Define an event struct with `#[event]` and `emit!(WithdrawEvent { user, amount, timestamp });` on every state-changing instruction. Events are part of Anchor's standard observability pattern.
findings.rule_caught: (non-rule-aligned — best-practice gap, not a security rule)
findings.root_cause: The silent_withdraw function performed a lamport transfer without emitting a structured Anchor event. Off-chain indexers, security monitors, and accounting systems cannot detect or react to the withdrawal, creating a blind spot in operational monitoring.
